news_communities.title: Local DAR chapter plans July 4 bell-ringing ceremony to begin Saugus Celebrates America at the Iron Works
news_communities.author: Jim Mitchell
news_communities.summary:
By Mark E. Vogler

 

The Parson Roby Chapter of the National Society Daughters of the American Revolution (NSDAR) will distribute bells for people attending tomorrow’s (July 4) America 250th celebration at the Saugus Iron Works National Historic Site as part of the nationwide “Ring Bells at Noon on July 4” initiative. Sponsored in part by the local NSDAR chapter, the old-fashioned patriotic gathering will be held from noon to 4 p.m. and is open to the public for free.

A reenactor dressed in a colonial costume as Parson Joseph Roby – who served as a Saugus pastor for more than half a century and who led 62 men from Saugus to Lexington and Concord on April 19, 1775 – will portray the reading of the Declaration of Independence. Members of the Children of the American Revolution (C.A.R.) will honor those patriots by reading the names of the men who answered the call to service. The celebration will recreate the spirit of 1776 with period games, food and colonial attire, along with photo opportunities featuring George and Martha Washington cutouts and period costumes. Winners of the chapter’s K–5 “What Freedom Means” writing and drawing contest will also be announced and recognized with prizes. Throughout the event, the Parson Roby Chapter will host a NSDAR information table to share the Society’s mission of historic preservation, education and patriotism.

Here is a tentative schedule for tomorrow’s event at the Iron Works:

From noon to 4 p.m. – BBQ (hot dogs, rolls, condiments, baked beans, lemonade, water, ginger cookies, cakes. DJ Anthony Arone will provide entertainment throughout the event. There will be a photo area with cutouts of George and Martha Washington and others.

Noon – Ringing of the Bells – Daughters of the American Revolution, Color Guard Presentation

Master of Ceremonies – Steve Castinetti – Pledge of Allegiance

National Anthem – Mary Beth Maes

Opening invocation – Pastor Wayne Shirk

Presentation of Citations – State Rep. Donald Wong and Saugus Board of Selectmen Chair Debra Panetta

Children’s games throughout Iron Works: Tug of war, three-legged race, sack races, corn hole, ring toss.

1 p.m. – General George Washington

2 p.m. – Parson Roby Narration; Reading of the Saugus Men who marched to Lexington; reenactment of the signing of the Declaration: John Hancock will read his story leading up to the Revolution and Ben Franklin will read a modern version of the Declaration of Independence.

3:30 p.m. – Presentation of winners to: “What Freedom Means to Me” writing and drawing contest; winner of Best Decorated Truck.

4 p.m. – Program concludes

A Shuttle for Senior Citizens – they can gather at the Saugus Senior Center at 11:45 a.m. and leave promptly in the shuttle to the Iron Works (if more than 12 people are there, a second shuttle will be available. A return shuttle from the Iron Works to the Senior Center will be made at 3:45 p.m.

Heat advisory – temperatures are expected to reach 95 degrees on Saturday. There will be several tents set up for people to get under for relief from the sun. The public is advised to keep hydrated and bring sunscreen to the event.

The event goes on, rain or shine. There is no rain date. Organizers hope for a rain-free day.

news_communities.title: The Imperio Mariense Feast prepares for July Fourth weekend in Saugus
news_communities.author: Jim Mitchell
news_communities.summary:
If you enjoy Portuguese food and entertainment, the Imperio Mariense Feast will be held this weekend, beginning tomorrow (Saturday, July 4) and concluding on Sunday (July 5th) at 262 Lynn Fells Parkway, Saugus.

The feast is an annual Portuguese cultural celebration which will be celebrating its 99th year, honoring the Divino Espírito Santo (Holy Ghost) tradition brought from the Azores in 1927. The event is being organized by the Imperio Mariense de Saugus, a non-profit group dedicated to preserving Azorean heritage through music, food, and community events.

The feast draws Portuguese natives or descendants from all over Massachusetts. Saugus selectmen granted organizers a temporary liquor license so guests can be served from 3 pm to Midnight on July 4 and from 11 am to 9 pm on July 5.

Imperio Mariense is the annual feast held in Saugus to celebrate the coronation of the Holy Ghost emperor and empress. Custom starts with a procession, followed by mass and coronation before the Holy Ghost soup lunch. The Holy Ghost celebration is specific to Azorean natives, established to reinforce a sense of community between the islands.

The two-day event is free and the entertainment features folkloric dance groups, live bands and cultural performances. The traditional Portuguese food includes malassadas, bifanas, iscas de fígado, and favas.

news_communities.title: National Grid Gas Main work begins on Central St. and Lincoln Ave.
news_communities.author: Jim Mitchell
news_communities.summary:
National Grid began a gas main project at various locations throughout town this week.

The Saugus Department of Public Works announced National Grid began gas main work involving the installation of 260 linear feet of new gas main at 166-180 Central St. The project will include a section of roadway that crosses over the Saugus River.

Work will occur between 7 a.m. and 3 p.m. on Monday through Friday, and the target project completion date is approximately July 31.

The utility was also scheduled to commence additional gas main work Wednesday (July 1) with 1,500 linear feet of new gas main at 386-413 Lincoln Ave. and 6-20 Kent Street. Work will occur between 8 a.m. and 2 p.m. on Monday through Friday, and the target project completion date is approximately September 15, 2026.

Police details will be used, and traffic detours will be established if necessary.

Please call Town Engineer Kevin Hallion or the Engineering Department at 781-231-4032 with any questions related to the National Grid gas main projects.

news_communities.title: MSBA Announces Final Audit of 10 School Projects totaling over $153.8M
news_communities.author: Jim Mitchell
news_communities.summary:
BOSTON—State Treasurer Deborah B. Goldberg, Chair of the Massachusetts School Building Authority (“MSBA”), and Mary L. Pichetti, MSBA Executive Director/Deputy CEO, announced this week that the MSBA Board of Directors has approved final audits for 10 projects from eight districts, totaling over $153.8 million in grants from the MSBA.

The final audit stage is the last step in the MSBA building process and represents the successful completion and closeout of these projects.

The Saugus Middle-High School, which was completed in August 2021, was among the projects that received final audit approval as a Core Program. Total Project Budget cost $160,691,190 The town received a Total Facilities Grant of $62,106,371.

“Every project undertaken by the MSBA is a step toward creating the 21st century learning environment that all Massachusetts students deserve,” Treasurer Goldberg said. “We are delighted to have made these important investments in Massachusetts schools.”

The Board approved the projects from the MSBA’s Core Program and Accelerated Repair Program, which consists of repairs or replacement to roofs, windows/doors and/or heat pumps. The combined grant value of the 5 audited projects was $153,898,024.

“Approval of the final audits brings these vitally needed projects to a successful conclusion, which benefits students all across the Commonwealth,” said Executive Director Mary Pichetti.

The MSBA partners with Massachusetts communities to support the design and construction of educationally appropriate, flexible, sustainable, and cost-effective public-school facilities. Since its 2004 inception, the Authority has approved over 1,200 projects and has made over $18.8 billion in reimbursements for school construction projects across the Commonwealth.

news_communities.title: The Sounds of Saugus
news_communities.author: Jim Mitchell
news_communities.summary:
By Mark E. Vogler

 

Good morning, Saugus

And happy Fourth of July to all of our readers and the residents of Saugus, as we prepare to observe and celebrate the 250th anniversary of the signing of the Declaration of Independence.

Ever since I can remember going back to my childhood days growing up in Swansea, July 4 has always been a special holiday. There was the giant barn fire in Fall River on the night before the Fourth. I saw my share of fireworks lighting up the skies over the Taunton River, whether watching it from a beach area in Somerset or in Fall River.

And my dad used to take my two brothers and me to the nation’s oldest and longest continuing Fourth of July Parade in Bristol, Rhode Island. The parade has been running since 1785 – two years after the Revolutionary War ended – and this year will be the 241st. What I remember most about that parade is seeing the center line of the 2.5 mile parade route painted in red, white and blue.

There were, of course, illegal firecrackers, cherry bombs and sparklers brought back from Warren, R.I., which borders Swansea. The Swansea police knew it was going on, but tolerated it because practically every family with kids was doing it. What would the Fourth of July be without fireworks? Right?

But the way I was brought up, July 4 was special because of the event and the American heroes it honored. Swansea and Greater Fall River were a part of that. Swansea was and is a proud New England town.

So is Saugus, which is steeped deep in a rich history that marked the early days of this country, especially the Revolutionary War. I spent Monday afternoon this week for an hour or so walking around the Revolutionary War Cemetery, right before the Rotary, at the corner of Main and Central Streets. There’s a lot of local history buried in the old cemetery – highlighted by the tombstones of local Minutemen from Saugus, which was then part of Lynn. The most notable tombstone was the one for Parson Joseph Roby, the minister who led 62 men from Saugus to the Battles in Concord and Lexington on April 19, 1775, according to historical accounts.

After milling around in the cemetery, I walked over to the Saugus Public Library to do some research. I leafed through a copy of the Centennial Anniversary of Saugus (July 3-4-5, 1915) and read Horace H. Atherton, Jr.’s “History of Saugus” article. Atherton wrote about Rev. Roby and also about Saugus during the Revolution.

“From the fact that Parson Roby was active in a portion of the Revolutionary period it probably is not a violent assumption to say that he was instrumental in seeing to it that Saugus did her full duty in that period by sending forth such a large representation of men to participate in the stirring times of that historic era,” Atherton wrote.

Nearly 100, by some accounts. The names of 99 Revolutionary War soldiers are inscribed on the monument in the cemetery.

As part of my research, I also skimmed through a small book titled “The Saugus Chronicles,” coauthored by Marilyn Carlson and Elliott Hurvitz. Longtime Saugus writer and historian Janice Jarosz calls Carlson “a great historian.”

“She’s unbelievable. She knows everything about Saugus,” Jarosz wrote me in an email this week about the former Saugus woman who now lives in Peabody.

Carlson, who was honored as the woman “Person of the Year” during the 2006 Founders Day celebration in Saugus, apparently continues to be a source of historical information about Saugus. Copies of the patriotic coloring book that she authored in 2000, with illustrations by Bob Sacco and Elliott Hurvitz, will be distributed to kids attending the America 250th celebration at the Saugus Iron Works tomorrow.

Hey, Saugus parents. If you want your children to be better informed about the town they live in, and you don’t have plans for the July 4 weekend, go down to the Saugus Iron Works tomorrow. It’s also a chance for you to learn facts about Saugus’ early days that you may not even now. Pick up a copy of that coloring book, too.

 

The Lynnhurst Block Party starts today

There’s only one Fourth of July celebration that’s been going on in Saugus for more than a century. It’s the Annual Lynnhurst Block Party, which gets underway today (Friday, July 3) at the Dana H. Johnson Memorial Park, next to the former Lynnhurst Elementary School. This will be the 109th year for this rich, neighborhood tradition, which takes parts of two consecutive days to celebrate July 4. The Brian Maes Band, which has been performing at the event for years as a local favorite, will do a concert tonight and be around town tomorrow. Organizers Jaclyn and Derek Hickman, who took over as organizers of the event in 2019 to keep the tradition alive, have been working hard to make this year’s block party a success.

The playground is named after Dana H. Johnson, who in 1971, at age 18, lost his life saving two boys at a beach in Marblehead. A stone memorial near the entrance of the Lynnhurst School honors the teenage hero from Saugus.

This is a super July 4 event that packs food, fun and games into a festive two-day celebration with patriotic pride. Tonight, there will be the concert by Brian Maes. Tomorrow will feature sack races, three-legged races, bike decorating, hot dogs, pie and, of course, watermelon.

The Lynnhurst Elementary School has been closed for several years. But multiple generations of Lynnhurst students will be there to reminisce about old times and celebrate new ones.

The neighborhood has held the block party on the Lynnhurst Playground since the 1960s. Before that it was held at the old Upper Lynnhurst School and Fairmont Avenue and the corner of Cleveland and Jefferson Avenues. It was originally organized by the Lynnhurst Men’s Club with publicity fliers distributed by Boy Scouts.

These days, social media helps organize the neighborhood in the planning of this huge Fourth of July party. If you’re a Lynnhurst area resident or a Saugonian who wants to be a part of something special and Americana, you might want to check this event out. The Saugus Fire Department will probably be there to spray the kids with some water to keep them cool.

 

Extreme Heat warning

Town Manager Scott Crabtree is urging residents to take precautions, as the National Weather Service has issued an extreme heat warning for Saugus that began Wednesday morning and will extend until 8 p.m. on the Fourth of July. The National Weather Service has forecast dangerously hot conditions for July 1-4, with heat index values that could rise up to 112 degrees for much of Southern New England, including Saugus.

Here is a message from the Town Manager’s Office:

“The National Weather Service cautions that this will be a prolonged and dangerous heatwave with little relief at night, noting that heat-related illnesses increase significantly during extreme heat and high humidity events.

“Residents are encouraged to take precautions during the predicted extreme heat, such as:

  • Never under any circumstances leave children or pets alone in a vehicle
  • Drink plenty of fluids
  • Find an air-conditioned space or other cool spot for relief
  • Watch out for heat-related illnesses
  • Stay out of the sun

“Please check on your family, friends, and neighbors to make sure they are safe during the extreme heat. Heat stroke is an emergency! Call 911.

“Please contact us if you are in need of emergency services, including needing a cool spot for relief.

“We are here to help. Anyone in need of emergency services should contact Saugus Emergency Management (SEMA) at 781-231-4175, or call 911.

“Our Police, Fire, Emergency Management, and Public Works Departments and our emergency medical services are here to assist the residents of Saugus in any way we can. Please do not hesitate to call. Thank you and be safe.”

 

Saugus United Parish Food Pantry

The Saugus United Parish Food Pantry will be open today (Friday, July 3) from 9:30-11 a.m. at 50 Essex St. in the basement of Cliftondale Congregational Church. The food pantry welcomes all neighbors facing food insecurity on Friday mornings. Volunteers are also welcome. Please call the Food Pantry Office (781-233-2663) or go to the Website cliftondalecc.org for details.

 

C.H.a.R.M. Center closed July 4

The Town of Saugus C.H.a.R.M. Center for compost/recycling drop-off will be closed for the Fourth of July holiday on Saturday, July 4, and will reopen for recycling and compost services on Wednesday, July 8. Please contact Solid Waste/Recycling Department Director Scott Brazis at 781-231-4036 with any questions. Thank you for your cooperation.

 

This week’s “Shout Outs”

This week’s special praise goes to members of the Saugus 250 Celebration Committee, the Town of Saugus, the numerous volunteers and donors – businesses and individuals – who helped make tomorrow’s (Saturday, July 4) Saugus 250th Celebration at the Saugus Iron Works National Historic Site happen. Hats off to these community leaders, town officials, businesses, individuals and others – particularly former Saugus Selectman Corinne Riley, who worked hard to organize this event before leaving office last fall and chaired the Celebration Committee. Hopefully, this is one of those great townwide events that Saugus people will be talking about for years. I certainly plan to get down to Saugus to cover it and take photos tomorrow for next week’s paper.

As Editor of The Saugus Advocate for more than a decade, I decided that this week’s “Shout Outs” should be reserved for anyone connected with the planning and execution of this event. So, I requested a list from the committee of everyone involved. I received several versions and updates of the list before our newspaper deadline. And I want folks to know that in the event that somebody is missing from the list provided by the Celebration Committee, the committee has offered to provide the names of those people and groups overlooked for next week’s paper. The people who have an asterisk after their name will also be appearing in costume and will be a part of the history reenactments.

So, let’s give a huge Fourth of July “Shout Out” to the following folks: the Saugus 250th Celebration Committee; the Saugus Iron Works – Mike Parr; Corinne Riley*; Janice Jarosz*; Ellen Leclair*; Jean Swanson*; Gail Cassarino*; State Representative Donald Wong; Selectmen Deb Panetta* and Anthony Cogliano*; Daughters of the American Revolution (DAR)*; Pastor Wayne Shirk*; Ret. U.S. Navy Capt. Steve Castinetti; Mary Beth Mayes; Town Manager Scott Crabtree and Jeannie Meredith; Elementary ELA Instructional Coach Kristin Barclay; the staff of Saugus Cable Television; the Saugus Veterans Council/Randy Briand; DJ Anthony Arone; Laura Eisener and Paul Kenworthy*; Tom Raiche*; Anthony* and Karen* Speziale*; Joyce Vecchiarelli and Peter Rossetti; Chris and Sirlei Tarantino; Mary Mackenzie; Chris Riley; the Saugus Police Department; the Saugus Fire Department; the Saugus Health Department; the Saugus Senior Center; the Precincts 4 and 10 Town Meeting Members who submitted patriotic pick-up trucks that will be on display at the Saugus Iron Works; Webster First Federal Credit Union; Kayem Hot Dogs; Big Y; Socs Ice Cream; the Saugus Lions Club; Trader Joe’s – Saugus; J Pace – Saugus; Nevron Plastics; Engineering Alliance; Rick Salvo and the Goldberg Family Chiropractic; Dr. AJ Goldberg; Kowloon Restaurant; KLP Associates DBA Tax Shop; Sue Palomba – Mango Realty; Mark Panetta; Nino’s Plastering; Sherry Campbell and Marilyn Carlson – for her contributions of historical information about Parson Roby.

If anyone needs to be added to this list, please let us know.

 

Want to “Shout Out” a fellow Saugonian?

This is an opportunity for our paper’s readers to single out – in a brief mention – remarkable acts or achievements by Saugus residents or an act of kindness or a nice gesture. Just send an email (mv***@*****st.net) with a mention in the subject line of “An Extra Shout Out.” No more than a paragraph; anything longer might lend itself to a story and/or a photo.

 

Free Summer concerts series underway

You know it’s truly summer in Saugus when the Wednesday night crowds start to gather at the Saugus Iron Works National Historic Site, where they sing, clap and dance to the music. It’s that time of year again. Another summer of free outdoor concerts over an eight-week period at the Iron Works began this week (Wednesday, July 1).

This marks the fifth consecutive summer that the Saugus Public Library and the National Park Service are collaborating on a local outdoor event that seems to get more popular every year. The Saugus Cultural Council (a local arm of the Mass Cultural Council, a state agency) is also a key sponsor of the event. The concerts are free and open to the public. The concert series celebrates local musical talents in Essex County and creates opportunities for residents and visitors to engage with the park during evening hours.

Shows begin at 6 p.m. each Wednesday in July and August. People can bring blankets and chairs, snacks, etc. Don’t forget to bring a friend, too, to enjoy a couple of hours of music outdoors. The events take place on the stage behind the museum building, and spectators can settle down on the upper lawn. It is suitable for all ages. Restrooms are available until nearly 8 p.m. when the concerts conclude. If weather looks bad for any particular Wednesday, concertgoers are urged to check the library website (www.sauguspubliclibrary.org), which will update for cancellations on that day.

This year’s lineup features a combination of new bands and familiar faces. Concerts begin at 6 p.m. on the Big Lawn each Wednesday through August 19. Atwater~Donnelly, a traditional American and Irish folk, percussive dancing group, opened the series this week. Here is the rest of the schedule for the next two months:

  • July 8: Brian Maes Band (Classic Rock).
  • July 15: Renee Leavitt & the Renegades (Lively Country/Rock Blend).
  • July 22: Memorylaners (50s, 60s and 70s, Doo Wop).
  • July 29: Ditto Band (Acoustic American Rock).
  • August 5: The Implications (Rock & Country).
  • August 12: Squeezebox Stompers (Cajun, Zydeco, Blues, Folk).
  • August 19: Coco Brown & The Invitation (Motown & Soul).

Watch https://sauguspubliclibrary.org for weather updates and cancellations.

 

Summer Reading time at the library

The 2026 Summer Reading Program at the Saugus Public Library is underway and will last through Friday, Aug. 21. Preregistration using the Beanstack app has begun this week. Here’s a summary of the program:

WHO: Kids Pre-K through 6th grade.

WHAT: Kids read the books they love, log their reading and earn prizes! They’ll enjoy reading what they love, free programs, crafts and activities.

WHY: Don’t lose those hard-earned skills! Summer reading plays a vital role in helping reduce the “Summer Slide” – the learning loss experienced between school years, which can leave students significantly behind their peers. The Saugus Public Schools recommend kids read at least 20 minutes every day. We also recommend reading to your child, even as they become an independent reader. Talk about what you’ve read together. Help them find books they like – we can help with that. Make it a fun part of a daily routine they look forward to!

PRIZES: Every reader wins prizes, and books to keep. We’ll also raffle vouchers for ice cream, pizza, roller skating, mini golf, bowling and much more. Grand prize drawings include tickets to Canobie Lake Park and Nova Adventure Park!

HOW: Download the Beanstack App!

  • CREATE AN ACCOUNT: If this is your first time participating in one of the SPL’s reading challenges using Beanstack, here are step-by-step instructions [https://www.loom.com/share/0efe0cc7c0f64b96b3c0a38ade8b04f6] on how to create a Beanstack account. If you’ve used Beanstack before, simply register for our 2026 Summer Reading Challenge.
  • REGISTER FOR A CHALLENGE: This video [https://www.loom.com/share/9cfd52f03a4c4444bc9ab86d94b51cea] will show you how to register for a reading challenge using the app.
  • LOG YOUR READING: This video [https://www.loom.com/share/fda67bebf80e443faaad2529fa3ca486] will show you how to log your reading using the mobile app.
  • FROM A COMPUTER: If you prefer not to use the app, here’s a link to access the SPL’s 2026 Kids Summer Reading Challenge using a computer. Beanstack Link [https://sauguspubliclibrary.beanstack.com/reader365]
  • Or Register in Person: Stop by the library to register in person and use our paper reading log.
  • Download free e-books and e-audiobooks from the library!

Can’t make it to the library? Our digital collections provide easy access to the books kids love, 24 hours a day seven days a week. Download free, fresh titles all summer using the hoopla and Libby apps! [https://www.sauguspubliclibrary.org/children/family-resources/]

 

KOC Crafts Fair Aug. 8

The Saugus Knights of Columbus No. 1829 will host a crafts fair Saturday, Aug. 8, from 9 a.m. to 3 p.m. at 57 Appleton St. in Saugus: baked goods, candles, handmade textiles, jewelry, wood carving and much more. Visitors can also enjoy making their own sundaes, or popcorn, hot dogs, hot coffee and donuts and raffles.

 

Veterans Day Brick Program is back

The Saugus Veterans Council is pleased to reinstate the Brick Program! After our vendor retired last year (thank you, Del from Pavlok for working with us over the years), we have found another engraver who will be taking our orders for Veterans Day! Proceeds from the brick sales are devoted to the upkeep of Saugus Veterans Park. If you are interested in purchasing a brick, a 4×8″ is $100 (three lines) and an 8×8″ (five lines) is $200. Please contact Corinne Riley, 781-231-7995, for an application or more information. All orders must be in no later than Sept. 10.

news_communities.title: Saugus Gardens in the Summer
news_communities.author: Jim Mitchell
news_communities.summary:
Here’s what’s blooming in town this week to make your walks more enjoyable

 

By Laura Eisener

 

Many gardeners have been inspired by the 250th anniversary to add patriotic decorations to their gardens this year, but one of the most spectacular is the Lucey home on Summer Street. Three window boxes easily visible from the sidewalk and street have been decked out in red, white and blue flowers and embellished with flags and other seasonal decorations. Maureen says she usually prefers a more pastel color palette, such as pinks and yellows, but this year for the semiquincentennial she decided to go for red, white and blue. The theme is continued in pots near the front walk, flowers in the beds and wreaths on the door. The hummingbirds have even been invited to the party with a bright red trimmed hummingbird feeder above one of the window boxes near the driveway.

As I mention nearly every 4th of July, true blue flowers are not always easy to find. The spreading plant we usually call annual lobelia (Lobelia erinus) comes in several shades of true blue as well as purple, pink and white. Its five petals are not all the same shape, as two upper ones are shorter than the three lower petals. They are very popular with pollinators, but tend to be avoided by deer and, usually, rabbits. Something did come along and eat the ends off my lobelias that had trailed beyond their pot. I did not catch the culprit in the act. In very tropical climates, this plant is perennial, but our Massachusetts winters are far colder than this species can endure. While it flowers best in a sunny location, it prefers moist but not soggy soil, so is likely to require more frequent watering than companions like petunia, million bells and geraniums enjoy.

Some of the containers also include a bright scarlet sage (Salvia coccinea), often simply called red salvia. It was one of my grandmother’s favorites, and she would plant it every summer in her backyard garden off of Makepeace Street. Like most species of sage, this long blooming one from South America has a scent in its leaves that discourages rabbits and deer from making it a meal. Deadheading faded flowers will encourage more blossom production as summer goes on. While most varieties available are bright red, it also can have pink, white, magenta or burgundy colors. Some varieties like the hybrid ‘Roman Red’ can get as tall as three feet in bloom, but most are one to two feet tall. One variety, Salsa Scarlet Bicolor, only reaches one foot tall, but it has red and white blossoms in the same flower spikes.

Red and white geraniums (Pelargonium hortorum) also make appearances in Maureen’s window boxes, but by far the most abundant are mini petunias (Petunia spp.), million bells (Calibrachoa spp.) and perhaps intergeneric hybrids between the two (Petchoa spp.). These fairly explode from all three window boxes as well as pots and beds. Million bells were once considered types of petunias but have for several decades been considered separate genera due to differences in DNA. Both are in the nightshade family (Solanaceae) and share some chemical similarities to tobacco (Nicotiana tabacum).

news_communities.title: Birthdays are special at the Saugus Senior Center
news_communities.author: Jim Mitchell
news_communities.summary:
(Editor’s Note: Saugus Senior Center Director Laurie Davis submitted the following article.)

 

The Saugus Senior Center recently held its monthly June Birthday Celebration, with 19 birthday celebrants joining us for a wonderful afternoon of fun, pizza and cake!

This month’s celebration was especially memorable as we honored Annette Slocomb on her 103rd birthday. Annette made the occasion even more meaningful by generously sponsoring the June Birthday Celebration.

Thank you, Annette, for your kindness, generosity and the joy you bring to our center. You are truly a remarkable lady, and we are so blessed to have you as part of the Saugus Senior Center family.

If you would like to sponsor one of our monthly birthday celebrations in honor of a loved one, please stop by the office for more information. We’d love to help you make someone’s birthday extra special.

news_communities.title: If Saugus were to have a Freedom Trail…
news_communities.author: Jim Mitchell
news_communities.summary:
There are a few landmarks and sites dating back to the Revolutionary War that would make it possible to have a walking tour around town

 

By Mark E. Vogler

 

Boston has its iconic Freedom Trail, which spans two and a half miles throughout the city, giving those who choose to walk it a taste of the city’s history, with many of the sites focusing on the American Revolution. Saugus could have its own Freedom Trail, too, as there are a number of landmarks and sites around town with direct ties to the birth of a new nation declaring its independence 250 years ago tomorrow.

In preparing this week’s edition we put this question to a handful of local historians as well as members of the Saugus 250th Celebration Committee:

“If somebody were to do a freedom trail in Saugus, where would the stops be?”

A consensus answer from the Saugus historians and history buffs who responded to The Saugus Advocate was the Revolutionary War Cemetery.

Longtime town historian and writer Janice Jarosz noted this comment from the Saugus Historical Commission dated June 2005:

“Revolutionary War Cemetery

“The tombstone of Parson Joseph Roby and many other early Saugonians are contained in this historic burial ground. Located at the intersection of Main and Central Street, the Cemetery was associated with the Third Parish Church when Saugus became an independent town in 1815.

“The church originally stood where the Civil War Monument now stands, and the cemetery extended from there to the central walkway. In the late 1800s, the development of the rotary island around the monuments resulted in the relocation of many graves from the roadway areas to the space to the west of the central walkway.”

Jean Swanson, a member of the Saugus Historical Commission and a member of the Saugus 250th Celebration Committee, texted us a copy of “Saugus Center, A Historic Walk,” produced by the Saugus Historical Commission in September 2015. The pamphlet mentioned the old cemetery with the exact wording. The cemetery was the only site on the walking tour talk from the American Revolution era.

Laura Eisener, president of the Saugus Historical Society, who writes “The Saugus Gardens” column for The Saugus Advocate, offered several ideas for sites that should be on a Saugus Freedom Trail. She mentioned the monument in the old cemetery, which includes the monument that lists the minutemen who went to Concord/Lexington on April 19. As another, she mentioned the marker on a stone in the rotary in Cliftondale put up by the DAR to the “Sons of Saugus.”

“Probably others have suggested those two locations, but here are two slightly less obvious – Appleton’s Pulpit which was the site of a rebellious speech well before the fighting actually broke out in Lexington, and Saugus Ironworks because without this industrial capacity the Colonies would be severely dependent on Europe,” Eisener continued.

Eisener offered these as possible candidates:

  • “The original meeting house/church opened in 1750 with Parson Joseph Roby as its minister from 1750-1803. It was approximately where the Civil War Monument stands today. It was an intersection rather than a rotary at that time.
  • “Ironworks House AKA Appleton Taylor Mansfield House – built in 1685 or so, was still owned by the Appleton family but not occupied by them. There may have been tenants – factory or farm workers – living in the house during the Revolution. Later the Mansfield Family took it over. 244 Central St. At Saugus Ironworks National Park. Ironworks not in operation by the time of the Revolution and industrial buildings dismantled before then.
  • “The Boardman House at 17 Howard St., owned by the Historic New England was built in 1692 and at the time of the revolution was occupied by Aaron Boardman and his family and residents probably included an enslaved man named Mark. This house was owned by descendants of the Boardmans until the early 20th century. Several Boardmans served in the Revolution.
  • “Near where Lincoln Ave and Ballard St. meet was the Rising Sun Tavern, and also a spring where Washington later stopped as they traveled between Boston and the North Shore when Washington visited the states after his election as President, and then in 1824 when Lafayette made his return visit, when he laid the cornerstone of the Bunker Hill Monument. The exact spot is not known as far as I can tell and there are no markers.”

Precinct 10 Town Meeting Member Peter Manoogian, a longtime participant in projects spotlighting the history of Saugus, didn’t make any recommendations for a Saugus Freedom Trail list. He instead offered an analytical response. “The values of independence, human dignity, and pursuit of happiness expressed in the Declaration of Independence are visible throughout the history of Saugus. The Scottish indentured servants who came to work in the Iron Works as a means to gain their freedom or the most recent newcomers to Saugus seeking a better life exemplify those values,” Manoogian said.

“While our neighbors are changing, the values that newcomers bring to Saugus are the same. Look around you at your neighbors, how hard they work as clerks, contractors, and small business owners. Saugus history, in my view, is not what ‘was’ but rather what ‘is,’ an enduring theme of independence, self-reliance, in our uniquely Saugus way to pursue happiness,” he said.

Readers are welcome to share their views on what a Revolutionary War-themed Freedom Trail in Saugus should include. Email me at mv***@*****st.net.

news_communities.title: Fourth of July at The Iron Works
news_communities.author: Jim Mitchell
news_communities.summary:
Beginning tomorrow at noon, Saugus will celebrate the nation’s 250th birthday with a patriotic party that will focus on the town’s role in the War for Independence

 

By Laura Eisener

 

Saugus played a distinctive and significant role in the War for Independence and we who live here would do well to reflect on it and appreciate our town’s importance in history.

The Saugus Iron Works National Historic Site will host an America 250th celebration of the signing of the Declaration of Independence tomorrow (Saturday, July 4) from noon to 4 p.m. This celebration will focus on the town’s role in events leading up to and during the American Revolution.

Well before any shots were fired in Lexington and Concord, Saugus played a different (though no less noisy!) role, as the development of vital industry was necessary before the colonies could pull away from its dependence on products from Europe. The deafening thuds from the trip-hammer and the grinding of gears in the slitting mill at the Iron Works, in addition to being necessary for the products actually made here, also helped train the many workers who fanned out to other towns in New England and beyond to set up new ironworks and factories.

Some of these workers were indentured servants from Scotland who might well be expected to pass on their displeasure with English rule to their descendants. Without the farming tools, nails and other items being made at Saugus Iron Works and the skills learned through the process, from refining ore to shipping the finished goods, activities from farming to town building, supplying weapons for troops, and other necessities would not have been easily available to residents of the New World.

In 1687, about eight years before the shot heard round the world was fired in Concord, Major Samuel Appleton’s speech on the projecting rock known as “Appleton’s Pulpit” in Saugus denounced the tyrannical behavior of Sir Edmund Andros, the colonial governor at the time.

At the time of the Revolution, what we now call the town of Saugus was part of the town of Lynn. Those who lived in the third parish, or west parish of Lynn, as the area was often called, had established their own church at what we now consider Saugus Center, and therefore were not legally required to go into Lynn Common every Sunday. As feelings against the rule of the king grew, many residents talked in the taverns of what the future might bring and began to anticipate that serious fighting might break out. On what is now known as Ballard Street, one tavern in particular was known as a gathering place for news of rebellion and ways to respond quickly.

In 1773, Jacob Newhall had bought the long-established Anchor Tavern in East Saugus from its unpopular British-born owner and immediately removed the lion and unicorn sign with one more symbolic of a new dawn, an era of enlightened independence and rejection of British domination not only for the town but all the colonies. He renamed the tavern the Rising Sun tavern and the sign depicted the sun rising above the horizon.

At this tavern the popular minister of the third parish church, Parson Joseph Roby, was among the men who enthusiastically embraced the idea of liberty. One of his sons had been among those who heaved boxes of tea overboard during the Boston tea party. When it was learned that the British were marching toward Concord and Lexington on April 18, 1775, over 60 men met at 3 a.m. on April 19 to make their way to meet militiamen from other towns to protect the threatened towns.

Captain David Parker’s Company consisted entirely of men from what is now Saugus. Other Companies in Colonel Timothy Pickering’s Regiment came from other parts of Lynn, but Parker’s Company was the largest and soon joined with other Companies in Arlington heading toward Concord. Most of the men were farmers, but there were also fishermen and men with other occupations, including Parson Roby. Lt. John Poole, Ensign Nathan Hawks, Sgt. Thomas Hawks, Sgt. Nathaniel Hutchinson, Sgt. Benjamin Redden, Corporal Abner Cheever Jr., Corporal Amos Leeds, Corporal Samuel Mansfield and Corporal Jabez Newhall were the officers under Captain Parker. The Company had a fifer, William Hill, and a drummer, Thomas Barrey. Allowing for the variable spellings of names that was common during this period, such as Hawks/Hawkes, Stoker/Stocker, Bordman/Boardman and Rhoads/Rhodes, many of these families are familiar as they or their descendants continued to develop businesses and participate in the Civil War and later conflicts up to the present day.

By the time the Revolution ended in 1783, 99 men from our town had fought. Many came home and continued to serve their town, which in 1815 separated from Lynn and resumed the name of Saugus, which had been used by Native Americans before the change to Lynn in 1634. Abijah Cheever was 15 when the Battles of Lexington and Concord broke out, but he helped make bullets the night before so his brothers would have ammunition when they went off to fight.

After graduating from Harvard College in 1779 and receiving his medical degree in 1782, he joined the navy as a surgeon on the Massachusetts ship Tartar. When the ship was captured by the British, he and his shipmates became prisoners detained in New York Harbor. After his release at the end of the war, he set up a medical practice in Boston for a few decades and raised a family, then returned to what had now become the town of Saugus and turned to politics. He was elected to the first Board of Selectmen and later to the Massachusetts House of Representatives. His younger brother Joseph also went into politics and in 1816 became the first town treasurer and was elected seven times to the Massachusetts House of Representatives. The Cheever home still stands on Vine Street.

Many of the families sent more than one son to battle. Sometimes fathers and sons were both of an age to fight. The marker at the end of the path in the Saugus Center Cemetery (also sometimes called the west parish cemetery or third parish cemetery, or in recent years the Revolutionary War cemetery) “erected by the Senior Citizens Association to Honor our Soldiers of the American Revolution” lists seven members of the Hitchings family, six Newhalls and six Boardmans, as well as other families represented by more than one individual. Six from the Stocker family also served as the war dragged on. Many of these soldiers were eventually laid to rest in this cemetery, their gravesites often including flag holders donated by the Sons of the Revolution (SAR). Family members are also interred here in many cases, although the old stones are sometimes difficult to read.

Just a few days after the Concord fight, Parson Roby joined the “safety committee,” which had a key part in organizing the townsmen to be ready for action. It has often been said that he would appear in church carrying his bible in one hand and his musket in the other. I have never heard it mentioned which arm he used for each! Once the revolution had ended, he continued his ministry for many more years, serving 51 years in all. While he did not live to see Saugus become a separate town, the Daughters of the American Revolution local chapter is named after him, and the brick school in Saugus Center that later became the School Administration building was named the Roby School. He remains one of the more colorful characters from the revolutionary era of Saugus history. A reenactor portraying him will be among those expected at the Iron Works tomorrow afternoon.

news_communities.title: Revere Traffic Commission Roundup
news_communities.author: Jim Mitchell
news_communities.summary:
By The Advocate

The Traffic Commission worked through a long agenda of public hearings and motions for traffic and parking improvements at their meeting last week.

77 Reservoir Ave.

The commission began with a public hearing request for a handicapped person parking space at 77 Reservoir Ave. The request was approved unanimously.

Ensign Street parking

A public hearing on whether to prohibit parking on Ensign Street, from Hauman Street to Sullivan Street, was continued so the commission could collect more information. Parking director Zach Babo said typically parking would be restricted for the entire length of a street. Babo said his department would conduct a wo-week study on parking enforcement and violations to present at the next Traffic commission meeting.

Stop signs for Centennial Avenue

The commission approved two isolated stop signs for Centennial Avenue. The first is to slow eastbound traffic to Campbell Avenue. The second stop sign is for westbound traffic on Campbell Avenue.

Stop sign for Shirley Avenue

An isolated stop sign on Shirley Avenue, for eastbound traffic to Thornton Street was unanimously approved.

Resident parking only on Prospect and Reservoir Avenues

The commission approved a request to make Prospect Avenue, from Northeast Broadway to 74 Prospect Avenue Resident Sticker Parking Only 24 Hours a Day 7 Days a week. Also, Reservoir Avenue from 6 reservoir Ave. to 46 Reservoir Ave. was also approved for resident parking only. Residents from the neighborhood were at the meeting to explain how people heading for the airport park in their neighborhood taking spaces for days. Ward 4 Councillor Paul Argenzio said it wasn’t a parking problem; it was a housing problem. The influx of residents is making parking tight.

Limiting parking permits

The commission took up a motion presented by Ward 5 City Councillor Angela Guarino Sawaya that they consider establishing a regulation limiting the number of residential parking permits issued per address or unit to four with the stipulation that residents may apply for additional parking permits through an appeal process administered by the Parking Department.

Parking Director Zach Babo said he did not believe the commission had enough information to approve the proposal. Babo said additional data was needed to provide a good working system. Babo also questioned if the motion would include hard caps or fees. He suggested the motion be sent to the traffic working group for review and the commission agreed.

Handicapped Person Parking

The commission voted unanimously to hold public hearings on requests for handicapped parking spaces at 84 Florence Avenue and 178 Constitution Avenue.

School parking restrictions

The commission discussed a group of requests to restrict parking to staff only at city schools including Beachmont, Garfield, Hill, Lincoln, Paul Revere, Revere High and Rumney Marsh Academy. The restrictions would run Monday through Friday, 6 a.m. to 6 p.m. Vehicles will be Towed at the owner’s expense and there is no parking before, during or after snow events.

Police Chief Maria LaVita said the restrictions do not include summer. Commissioners understood the need for staff parking but suggested the restricted hours could be trimmed back and allowances made for parking during snowstorms. The commission will hold a public hearing on the proposals.

news_communities.title: Beyond Walls continues to support Lynn students
news_communities.author: Olivia HaydarOlivia HaydarOlivia Haydar
news_communities.summary:
LYNN — Beyond Walls goes above and beyond when it comes to their Classroom to the Streets program. 

For their third year running a fellowship program with Lynn Public Schools (LPS) and St. Mary’s School, the organization seeks students interested in careers in art or education, said Lisa McCarthy, Classroom to the Streets (CTTS) director. 

“We asked the high school art educators to look at their students and identify some that they think might be a good fit for the program,” McCarthy said. 

Classroom to the Streets is a fellowship program that connects Lynn students with their community and professional artists, providing an experience many students don’t get. 

“I have not seen anything similar to this program specifically, so it’s very unique and we’re really grateful that our students have this opportunity,” LPS Curriculum Director of the Arts, Dr. Patty Klibansky, said. “But also the teachers as well, because they’re learning alongside the students.” 

The students and teachers become immersed in the vibrant and diverse Lynn community, turning the streets into a live classroom experience, Klibansky said. 

When it comes to choosing students, teachers are vital to understanding who would thrive in the program because of the level of connection they build in the classroom. 

“The learning experience is interesting, you know, it’s actually for everybody involved,” McCarthy said. 

After the students are scouted by their educators, they’re instructed to apply to the program, McCarthy said. 

“There was a prescreening and application process, so students had to fill out a form that was really to help us get to know the students and see where their areas of focus are,” she added. 

Through this process, the Beyond Walls program leaders and artists can begin to form understandings of the students’ strengths and weaknesses, which later turns into a collective theme that represents the community as a whole. 

Beyond Walls also works to reach out to the community and schools to see whether there are groups who feel underrepresented, McCarthy said. In 2025, the organization asked LPS whether there was a segment of the population that felt forgotten in celebration, and the response was that the Italian community needed more tender loving care. 

The organization then engaged an Italian mural artist to help bring their sense of culture to the wall art, while also engaging with students who felt that population needed to be more visible, McCarthy said. 

“This year, one of the intentional artists that we sought out works with ceramics and does mosaics, and that was based directly on the feedback we got from one of the educators who thought students would really benefit from working with a tactile, hands-on pop art experience,” McCarthy said. “We’re always taking so many different variables into consideration.” 

To expand on what the program teaches students and educators on the street, lessons from the cohorts are then integrated into the schools’ art curricula, including field trips to murals painted that previous summer, Klibansky said. 

“So students are learning contemporary art from their teachers in real time about artists who are alive today,” Klibansky said. “I think that’s really important in the curriculum, that students can relate to the art that they’re looking at in some way.” 

One of the new initiatives Beyond Walls will add to their 2026 CTTS program is the inclusion of STEAM (Science, Technology, Engineering, Art, and Math) based lessons, McCarthy said. 

One thing Julia Midland, the director of communications and external relations, hopes families and students take away from the CTTS program is the power of working as a team. 

“We can do really great things when we work together, and for them (students) to come out and see the art and understand that it’s their art too,” Midland said. 

For Klibansky, it’s more about students making an unbreakable connection with their community through their artistic expression. 

“I think it’s extremely important that students have access to the work that’s right here in their own city, and that they understand and feel a connection to the city, and that they are learning not just about the community, but also from a curriculum standpoint… like understanding the elements of art and principles of design,” Klibansky said. 

The CTTS program has been solely in Lynn since the fellowship began in 2023, but Beyond Walls projects have expanded farther than the city lines, Midland said. Since their opening in 2017, the organization has worked within 18 different communities, she added, and there’s hopes for CTTS to expand alongside the organization. 

“Classroom to the Streets is only in Lynn right now, and I think we have had such great results and experiences in partnership with Lynn,” Midland said. “We want to continue it. We’ve definitely gotten interest from other places… and it might be a good thing for them to deepen their work… There’s no plan right now to go to another city… but we’re always open.” 

To build on past experiences, Beyond Walls will be incorporating mural walking tours into their bi-weekly calendar, Midland said. 

“We’re bringing back mural tours every other Saturday; now through the fall there will be public mural tours that people can sign up for,” Midland said. “We’ve had little stints of them over the years… but we’re really excited to have a more formal kind of mural tour season.” 

Helping promote youth’s art in the community has been a consistent effort to help grow their confidence to be professional artists, Midland shared. 

Some students have even reached back out to Beyond Walls for letters of recommendation, sharing they’ve been accepted to some of the nation’s most prestigious art schools, including SCAD, Mass Art, and Rhode Island School of Design, McCarthy said. 

“They are our future,” Midland said.

The post Beyond Walls continues to support Lynn students appeared first on Itemlive.

news_communities.title: Marblehead Festival of Arts goes forth
news_communities.author: Dylan PichnarcikDylan PichnarcikDylan Pichnarcik
news_communities.summary:
MARBLEHEAD — The sound of Abbott Hall’s bell chime at 6 p.m. on Wednesday not only signified the start of a new hour but also served as the opening chime for the 60th Marblehead Festival of Arts.

In the shadow of Abbott Hall’s historic facade, the winners of the 2026 Festival Awards were announced from each exhibit on display during the festival.

Each of the Festival’s exhibits is displayed in various locations around town. The award ceremony included recognition of “Best of the Show” winners.

The 2026 Marblehead Festival of Arts Best of Show winners were Diane Treadwell (Crafts); Amy Hourihan (Printmaking); Dawn Jenkins (Senior Art); Harper Mooney (Youth & Student Art); Kiki Taron Kinney (Sculpture); Leon Drachman (Paint the Town Exhibit); Yelena Kanevsky (Mixed Media); Ed Lewis (Digital Art); Ulrike Welsch (Photography – black and white); Glenn Engman (Photography – color); and Todd Zalewski (Drawing and Painting).

Three scholarships were awarded to Marblehead students at the ceremony.

Stanislav “Stan” Almeda, Sara “Sadie” Beane, and Quinn Scheib each received a $3,000 scholarship from the Festival of Arts.

According to the Festival of Arts website, “since 1986, the Marblehead Festival of Arts has awarded scholarships to high school seniors who reside in or attend schools in Marblehead and who are pursuing higher education in visual arts, performing arts, crafts, music, or drama. The Festival’s objective is to encourage students interested in pursuing the arts professionally to acquire a sound academic grounding in their chosen fields. The scholarship is awarded annually.”

The post Marblehead Festival of Arts goes forth appeared first on Itemlive.

news_communities.title: Candidates appeal ruling removing them from ballot
news_communities.author: Amanda LureyAmanda LureyAmanda Lurey
news_communities.summary:
Following the State Ballot Law Commission’s June 26 ruling that lieutenant governor candidate Anne Manning Martin, of Peabody, and attorney general candidate Michael Walsh, of Lynnfield, will not have their names printed on the Republican State Primary ballot due to fraudulent signatures, both candidates filed appeals.

In separate court filings, Manning Martin and Walsh argued the commission committed legal and procedural errors when it invalidated enough signatures from their nomination papers to leave each below the required 10,000 certified signatures needed to qualify for the Sept. 1 Republican primary ballot.

The commission determined that Manning Martin filed 10,692 certified signatures, and Walsh filed 10,677, but concluded that each campaign had submitted enough invalid signatures to fall short of the qualifying threshold.

The decisions stemmed from objections filed by Massachusetts Democratic Party Executive Director Adam Roof, and, in Manning Martin’s case, Republican Shawn Oliver, who is also vying for lieutenant governor.

During the hearings, the commission heard testimony that hundreds of signatures collected in Weymouth appeared in the same order as voter lists and, in some instances, matched the order of signatures submitted by other candidates.

The commission also considered evidence involving political consultant Joseph Bronske, who collected signatures for multiple Republican candidates, including Manning Martin and Walsh. According to the commission’s findings, Bronske invoked his Fifth Amendment right against self-incrimination during a deposition and declined to answer questions about whether he forged signatures or knew of anyone who had done so.

Ultimately, the commission invalidated 1,279 of Manning Martin’s signatures, leaving her with 9,413 valid signatures. It also invalidated 1,021 of Walsh’s signatures, leaving him with 9,656 valid signatures.

In her appeal, filed in Essex Superior Court on Wednesday, Manning Martin argued the commission exceeded its authority and violated her constitutional rights by allowing objections she said should have been dismissed on procedural grounds, including issues involving service by certified mail and disclosure of challenged signatures.

She also asserted that the commission relied on evidence that was not properly admitted, prevented her from fully presenting her defense, improperly shifted the burden of proof, and made mathematical errors. Manning Martin further argued even if the disputed signatures were removed, she still exceeded the 10,000-signature requirement and should appear on the ballot.

Walsh raised many of the same procedural objections in his Suffolk Superior Court appeal, which was filed on Tuesday. He argued the commission wrongly accepted Roof’s challenge despite what Walsh maintained were defects in service and filing requirements. He also contended the commission improperly allowed a registered Democrat to challenge a Republican Party’s constitutional right to choose its own nominees without interference from political opponents.

Like Manning Martin, Walsh stated that the commission relied on insufficient evidence and legal errors when it removed him from the ballot.

Separately, on June 18, Walsh also sought emergency relief from the U.S. Supreme Court while the state court challenges continue.

Unless a court overturns the commission’s decisions before ballots are finalized, neither Manning Martin nor Walsh will appear on the Republican primary ballot.

The post Candidates appeal ruling removing them from ballot appeared first on Itemlive.

news_communities.title: Moulton to join Frederick Douglass reading
news_communities.author: Olivia HaydarOlivia HaydarOlivia Haydar
news_communities.summary:
LYNN – U.S. Rep. Seth Moulton will join the annual reading of Fredrick Douglass’ speech, “What to the Slave is the Fourth of July?” on Friday to celebrate Independence Day.

The North Shore Juneteenth Association runs the event, and it will take place at the Frederick Douglass Memorial Park on Exchange Street in Lynn from 11 a.m. to 2 p.m. to honor Douglass’ life story and efforts toward the abolitionist movement.

The speech read by Douglass on July 5, 1852, is a critique of American hypocrisy that remains significant through current national challenges.

The post Moulton to join Frederick Douglass reading appeared first on Itemlive.

news_communities.title: Lynn and Swampscott issue beach advisory
news_communities.author: Erin HickeyErin HickeyErin Hickey
news_communities.summary:
Residents are being advised to avoid swimming at King’s Beach and nearby coastal waters for at least 48 hours after Tuesday’s heavy rainfall, which triggered a combined sewer overflow into Nahant Bay. 

The advisory was issued Wednesday by the Swampscott Health Department and the Lynn Water & Sewer Commission and notified residents that combined sewer overflow had discharged untreated or partially treated sewage into Nahant Bay and King’s Beach during Tuesday’s storm. 

The message confirmed that the overflow had ceased at 5:55 p.m. on Tuesday, and warned residents to avoid contact with affected waters for 48 hours from that point because of increased health risks from bacteria and other pollutants. 

The notification came less than 24 hours after the Swampscott Board of Health spent much of its Tuesday evening meeting discussing how rainfall affects beach water quality and announcing that weekday testing had kicked off at Fisherman’s Beach the day before. 

The Fisherman’s testing supplements the town’s existing beach water testing program, which Public Health Director Jeff Vaughan conducts once a week at each of Swampscott’s six public beaches. The additional weekday testing is intended to provide a more detailed picture of water quality at a location that has drawn increased attention because of concerns about stormwater runoff. 

Vaughan, who runs his beach water testing once a week on Wednesdays, said that an elevated bacteria result would prompt him to test again the following day before issuing a closure of a beach. 

Officials also reemphasized that the water testing process takes roughly 24 hours and results are not immediately available to public health personnel. The board discussed its longstanding recommendation that residents stay out of the water for 48 to 72 hours after significant rainfall, when bacteria levels are most likely to increase. 

The board briefly discussed whether it was within their purview to define what might constitute a “major rain event,” though they ultimately determined such a thing was not within their limits and could not be narrowly defined. Board of Health Chair Gargi Cooper noted that officials have seen unsafe bacteria levels after brief rains, and sometimes not after major storms, making the risk difficult to quantify.

“If it has rained, we are telling people: avoid the water. It’s kind of as simple as that,” Cooper said. “I know that’s not as sophisticated as people want to hear, but we don’t have the equipment.” 

Allen also said that the college student intern administering the weekday testing at Fisherman’s will be collecting samples from both the Marshall Street and Cassidy Park culverts to help identify potential sources of bacterial contamination entering the harbor. The expanded monitoring follows infrastructure improvements completed over the past two years and should provide a much larger dataset to illustrate continued problems or (hopefully) progress. 

“A lot of work has been done in terms of IDDE infrastructure work in that area over the last two years,” Allen said. “And I think it’s been a couple of years since any meaningful culvert testing was done. So I think that will be very useful.” 

Allen did note that culvert testing would not happen every day, as the tester is sometimes taking samples in the morning during high tide. He said that sampling the culverts when the beach water levels are high could lead to misleading samples that are more ocean than culvert — something that he said might be problematic with current DCR culvert sampling data. 

The board also discussed next steps in making beach water testing data publicly available to residents and beachgoers. 

The Lynn and Swampscott combined sewer overflow notification included a link to a real-time online map showing the status of sewer overflows in the area. 

Cooper said the town was working toward making the new Fisherman’s Beach monitoring results publicly available online as they are collected throughout the summer. 

The results of Vaughan’s weekly testing are publicly available through the state’s Interactive Beach Water Quality Dashboard. Additional DCR testing data are available through SaveKingsBeach.com

The post Lynn and Swampscott issue beach advisory appeared first on Itemlive.

news_communities.title: Msgr. Paul V. Garrity: Compassion or cruelty
news_communities.author: Msgr. Paul V. GarrityMsgr. Paul V. GarrityMsgr. Paul V. Garrity
news_communities.summary:
It was no surprise when the Supreme Court determined that the present administration is within its rights to terminate Temporary Protected Status (TPS) granted to thousands of immigrants over the past 36 years. First signed into law in 1990 by President George H.W. Bush, TPS was envisioned to be a temporary humanitarian immigration program for people who were seeking refuge from ongoing armed conflict, environmental disasters, and other native land conditions that threatened their lives and safety. Today, there are anywhere from 300,000 to 1.3 million people in the U.S. enrolled in this program. Because the program was meant to be a temporary accommodation, the Court found that the administration could legally terminate TPS status and, presumably, begin deportation proceedings. 

Since 1990, successive administrations have allowed TPS recipients to live and work in the U.S. without fear of deportation. The recent Supreme Court decision came about because of the current administration’s decision to revoke TPS for all those who have lived and flourished in the U.S. under this humanitarian immigration exception. This decision clears the way for the potential deportation of individuals and families who have called the U.S. home for twenty years or more. 

In simple terms, these people have been contributing to the US economy to the tune of billions of dollars. They have become homeowners, taxpayers, and essential members of the fabric of our nation. They are employed in factories, restaurants, the building trades, nursing homes, and hospitals. In short, they have assimilated into the larger melting pot that is our nation. Their presence in the US does no harm but provides cadres of workers for positions that are often very hard to fill. Their departure will not only bring considerable harm to them but will also bring harm to our communities and to our economy. The implications of revoking TPS are catastrophic and cruel.

TPS was not created as a partisan win for Republicans or Democrats. It was created in a bipartisan way as an extension of the values synonymous with our history. “Give me your tired, your poor, your huddled masses yearning to breathe free, the wretched refuse of your teeming shore. Send these, the homeless, tempest-tost, to me. I lift my lamp beside the golden door!”  This inscription from the base of the Statue of Liberty is all about compassion and generosity. Revoking TPS is diametrically opposed to this late-19th-century statement of American ideals and values.

The larger question of which TPS is one aspect is the overall immigration policy of our nation today. Since 1790, immigration laws and policies have been a mixed bag. From an open-door policy for Western Europeans through race-based exclusion policies and complex quota systems, immigration laws have reflected the social temperature of our citizenry. In 2026, there is no broadly articulated consensus of what immigration reform should look like for the present and future. This absence allows those with the loudest voices to politicize immigration reform for narrow, political gain while at the same time underscoring the absolute need for new ways of dealing with the immigration challenges that surround us. 

Since 1990, both political parties have refused to address the clear and present conundrum that immigration reform demands. Revoking TPS is only the latest reminder that the status quo is doing no one any good. The paralysis we see in Washington is only the latest reflection of our nation’s social temperature.

As we celebrate the 250th anniversary of the Declaration of Independence, we need a revival of the best instincts of our forebears. The improvements in our nation have taken place because of the courage of people who stood against slavery, discrimination, sexism, jingoism, and isolationism. Instead of allowing demagogues to rule the day, courageous women and men have elevated compassion above cruelty, generosity above selfishness, and the common good above partisan interests. Overall, the recognition and pursuit for human dignity has been a core value for which so many have lived and died.

It is past time for the silent majority of compassionate Americans to rise up in defense of the values that have made our nation the beacon of freedom and opportunity that it has been. 

Msgr. Garrity is a Senior Priest of the Archdiocese of Boston and former pastor of St. Mary’s Parish and High School in Lynn.

The post Msgr. Paul V. Garrity: Compassion or cruelty appeared first on Itemlive.

news_communities.title: Jim Walsh: Rising? Setting?
news_communities.author: Jim WalshJim WalshJim Walsh
news_communities.summary:
In 1787, George Washington served as chair of the Constitutional Convention, presiding over 55 delegates from the 13 colonies. The Articles of Confederation had not worked out. Nobody was happy. The Revolution had not been as liberating as many had hoped. As presiding officer, sitting on a small, slightly raised stage, Washington occupied a grand, beautifully carved chair visible from the floor. On its back was carved the image of a half-sun rising. 

Or was it setting? 

Over the course of the Philadelphia convention, the 81-year-old Benjamin Franklin pondered that question. The direction of the Revolution’s Sun was not always clear. In the end, Franklin concluded that our Sun was on the rise even though, as he departed the gathering at Constitutional Hall on its final day, he maintained some uncertainty as to whether it would continue to do so.

Fifty years later, Frederick Douglass, an escaped slave, moved to Lynn, Massachusetts. It was a small city. On its edges were the restless neighborhoods called “Nahant” and “Swampscott.” He was an extraordinary man. The wife of his enslaver had started him on the road to reading until her husband forbade her to continue. But he was hooked. He continued to educate himself, often seeking help from befriended white kids in the process. By the time he escaped slavery, in 1838 at the age of 20, he was fully literate with the King James Bible as his central source of wisdom. His Sun was rising.

It was in Lynn that he wrote his autobiography. He befriended Wendell Phillips, a young abolitionist attorney active in Boston, who had a summer place in Nahant. He associated with abolitionist societies and became well-known as a speaker, a persuader, a communicator, an example of what an intelligent young man of whatever race could do. To many, he was a threat. As he became well-known, he became more fearful of being captured and returned to his “owners.” The abolitionists put him on a ship to England, where he continued his work to end slavery. Supporters there raised enough money to buy his freedom, making him a free man … of sorts. There were few truly free Black men in America at that time … if any.

In 1852, he was invited to speak in Rochester, New York, on the subject of “What, to the Slave, Is the Fourth of July?” That speech will soon be performed on Thursday, July 2, at 4 p.m. in the air-conditioned auditorium of Swampscott High School. Last year, it was performed in the shadow of a Monument to veterans of a Civil War yet to come, a monument by and to residents of those Lynn neighborhoods, Nahant, and Swampscott who had died fighting slavery.

Massachusetts was one of the first states to abolish and outlaw slavery. Its Supreme Judicial Court had ruled that slavery was unconstitutional and incompatible with the clause in the Massachusetts State Constitution of 1780… “all men are born free and equal.” It was a wonderful start, but it would be a long time before women would be fully included in that concept.

For some of us, it is the very essence of our democratic republic that progress is not only possible…it is required. It’s built into our amendable Constitution. Unfortunately, progress is not inevitable. We citizens must take it upon ourselves to work at it.

Frederick Douglass was a remarkable man, with a remarkable mind and spirit. I wonder what he would think now if he were here today? Could that 34-year-old man have imagined a gathering in an air-conditioned hall to remember him and recite his words?

In our Commonwealth, its free citizens elected a Black man of the Democratic Party, Deval Patrick, as governor of Massachusetts. Not once, but twice.

In our Commonwealth, its free citizens elected a Black man of the Republican Party as its Senator from Massachusetts in Washington, Edward Brooke. Not once, but twice.

As we listen to the words of Frederick Douglass in the cool comfort of Swampscott High School on July 2nd, we might well wonder if he thought a man of color would sit in the Massachusetts Governor’s office on Beacon Hill or would represent us in the halls of the United States Senate in Washington, D.C. One wonders what he might have thought about a gay woman sitting in the Governor’s office right now. 

And, ultimately, could he have foreseen Barack Obama raising his right hand, taking the oath of office as the President of the United States? Not once, but twice?

And what would he have thought upon learning that the first words of his famous speech would be recited by the first woman of color to be elected to statewide office in Massachusetts history…Attorney General Andrea Joy Campbell.

Did the election of Attorney General Campbell take too long? 

Possibly. Probably. But it happened.

Good change doesn’t happen overnight. It doesn’t follow a strict, straight road. There are potholes, dangerous curves, oncoming traffic, and sharp corners along that rocky route. But good change HAS happened…and Frederick Douglass would have much to be happy about…as do we.

America has evolved. It has improved. And it’s done so because we worked at it. The seeds of a fully just society were planted in the Constitution. They have been nurtured and grown over time. That’s what Amendments are for. In the 21st century, let them bloom. Protect them from harm.

Our Fourth of July this year should encourage us all to value, to protect, to defend, and to nourish that which we have achieved. Our job is not done.

Thank you for being here, Frederick Douglass. Thank you for your returning spirit. 

Welcome back, sir!

Jim Walsh is a writer who lives in Nahant.

The post Jim Walsh: Rising? Setting? appeared first on Itemlive.

news_communities.title: Saugus home invasion suspects plead not guilty
news_communities.author: Elizabeth Della PianaElizabeth Della PianaElizabeth Della Piana
news_communities.summary:
SAUGUS — Timothy Gregory, 55, of Boston, and Derek Matarazzo, 38, of Charlestown, were arraigned Wednesday in Essex County Superior Court regarding charges from a May 3 armed home invasion on Oakwood Avenue in Saugus, District Attorney Paul F. Tucker announced.

Calls came in just after noon that Sunday with reports of two masked men, dressed in all black and armed with handguns, breaking into a home on the street.

Police Chief Michael Ricciardelli had reported that shots had been fired, but no one was injured.

In a statement that day, the department noted that two men, Gregory and Matarazzo, were detained. Immediately after the arrest, the department stated that the two men were the only ones involved and that it was believed to be an isolated incident.

The men appeared on Zoom before the Hon. Salim Tabit, both pleading not guilty to home invasion, two counts of armed assault in a dwelling, two counts of assault with a dangerous weapon, larceny over $1,200, and carrying a firearm without a license.

Attorney Grace Edwards represents Gregory, and Joseph Smith represents Matarazzo.

Judge Tabit ordered that the defendants remain held without bail, without prejudice, pending the result of a detention hearing scheduled for July 29, according to the DA Tucker’s release.

All suspects and defendants are presumed innocent unless proven guilty in a court of law.

The post Saugus home invasion suspects plead not guilty appeared first on Itemlive.

news_communities.title: Jayne Saraceni
news_communities.author: Independent Staff
news_communities.summary:
She loved to be surrounded by her family

Jayne M. (Cardinale) Saraceni passed away on June 21st at 80 years of age.

She was born in Winthrop on April 20, 1946, and was the daughter of James and Mary (DeRosa) Cardinale. She was raised in East Boston during her early childhood and was educated there in the public school system. She was a graduate of East Boston High School. When she married Joseph “Moe” Saraceni, they moved to Chelsea where they raised their family.

Jayne worked for many years at Suffolk Downs where she held several positions. She worked in the accounts payable department before entering the security department. She loved to be surrounded by her family, especially her grandchildren and also enjoyed playing Bingo and card games in her spare time.

The beloved wife of the late Joseph R. “Moe” Saraceni and the devoted mother of Joseph V. Bocchetti and his wife, Lisa of Haverhill and James J. Bocchetti; the cherished grandmother of Marissa Grassi and her husband, Christopher and Ashley Bocchetti; dear sister of the late Joseph Cardinale, Dolly Mazza and Patricia Bavaro. She is also lovingly survived by many nieces and nephews.

Honoring Jayne’s wishes, funeral services will be held privately.

To send online condolences, please visit www.vertucciosmithvazza.com. Funeral arrangements entrusted to the care and direction of the Vertuccio Smith & Vazza, Beechwood Home for Funerals, Revere.
